catalog_summary.title: The Donet
catalog_summary.author: Reginald Pecock
catalog_summary.date: c. 1445; composed within the broader range 1443-1449
catalog_summary.region: Mainly East Midland language; exact place of composition unresolved

cataloging_subject.label: The Donet
cataloging_subject.scope_note: Reginald Pecock's two-part Middle English prose dialogue introducing and summarizing Christian doctrine, ethics, prayer, and meditation. The subject includes the work's prologue and two parts as preserved in Bodleian Library MS. Bodl. 916, but excludes that physical codex, Hitchcock's 1921 edition and apparatus, the separate authorial abridgement The Poore Mennis Myrrour, the University of Michigan encoding, and the repository XML artifact.

notes: The manuscript has no heading that directly supplies a title, but the prologue calls the dialogue a 'donet' or key, and the institutional catalog and scholarly edition use The Donet.

titles.value: Þe donet or key of goddis lawe; þe donet or key of cristen religioun
The donet, by Reginald Pecock. Now first edited from MS. Bodl. 916 and collated with The poore mennis myrrour (British Museum, Addl. 37788) by Elsie Vaughan Hitchcock

scope.description: U-M item title incorporating edition and source information

titles.value: For as moche as the book y-callid the reule of cristen religioun

scope.description: Opening of the English prologue after the Latin invocation

agents.name: Reginald Pecock
notes: Bishop of St Asaph from 1444 and of Chichester from 1450; the surviving codex itself lacks a contemporaneous direct attribution, but the work is securely cataloged and edited as his.

agents.name: Elsie Vaughan Hitchcock
notes: Editor of the 1921 first edition and collator of parallel passages in The Poore Mennis Myrrour.

The current Bodleian catalog identifies Pecock as author. Hitchcock also supports the attribution through the work's relations to Pecock's other writings while noting that the manuscript has no direct original author heading.

scope.description: The 1921 scholarly transcription, punctuation, emendation, notes, and apparatus used by the U-M encoding
Parallel passages in Part I compared with The Poore Mennis Myrrour

date_statements.display: c. 1445; probably composed and authorized within 1443-1449

assertion.notes: Hitchcock argues that authorized publication followed the Rule of Christian Religion after 1443 and probably preceded work on the Repressor in 1449, while acknowledging that no exact year can be assigned. The Middle English Dictionary conventionally labels the work c. 1445.

date_statements.display: Bodleian Library MS. Bodl. 916, second half of the fifteenth century

scope.description: The sole known surviving manuscript copy

places.label: East Midlands, England
notes: Hitchcock describes Pecock's language as mainly early-fifteenth-century East Midland and treats a more specific Oxford-dialect proposal as plausible but unproven.

scope.description: The linguistic character of Pecock's work as represented by the sole later copy

assertion.notes: A dialect region does not establish an exact place of composition, and the surviving copy may mix authorial and scribal features.

notes: Do not infer composition at Oxford merely from the proposed Oxford dialect or the manuscript's present Bodleian location.

language_statements.label: Latin

scope.description: The opening invocation, technical terminology, work titles, and scriptural or learned phrases embedded in the Middle English prose

notes: Brief embedded Latin does not make the work a bilingual parallel text.

form_statements.description: A sustained didactic prose dialogue between a son asking questions and a father answering. Brief Latin phrases and quoted formulas do not make the work mixed-form.

assertion.notes: The work teaches through a father-and-son question-and-answer frame, though its extended arguments are more elaborate than a short elementary catechism.

summaries.value: A two-part Middle English prose dialogue in which a son questions a father about Christian religion. Pecock presents it as an introductory key, compact report, and complement to his longer Rule of Christian Religion. The prologue explains the project and title; Part I treats the soul, reason and free will, natural and moral philosophy, virtues, vices, and the seven matters of Christian living; Part II develops further doctrinal and ethical questions and closes with sustained instruction in vocal prayer and meditation. The sole surviving manuscript is imperfect, with material lost around its second part.

content_parts.label: Prologue
content_parts.summary: Explains the Donet as an introduction, compendium, and complement to the Rule of Christian Religion; defines its father-and-son dialogue form and two-part plan; explains the title; defends writing in English; and asks readers to judge the author's intended meaning fairly.

content_parts.label: Part I
content_parts.summary: Eighteen chapters introduce the soul, bodily and rational faculties, natural and moral philosophy, the four tables of moral virtues, the seven matters necessary to Christian living, and related instruction on law, virtue, vice, belief, and conduct.

content_parts.label: Part II
content_parts.summary: Twenty-three surviving chapter containers develop further truths within the seven matters, defend the work's arrangement, and culminate in practical instruction on praise, prayer, meditation, and disciplined Christian activity.
notes: The witness lacks four leaves in its second part and probably loses at least one chapter heading. Hitchcock therefore questions the numbering of chapters 14-18; the 23 XML containers describe the surviving edited structure, not a secure count of the originally composed chapters.

extent.description: The surviving work is organized as one prose prologue, eighteen Part I chapter containers, and twenty-three Part II chapter containers. The treatise division of CME00008.xml contains 642 P, 214 PB, 2,299 NOTE, and 14,114 HI elements and no L or LG elements. These are encoding metrics. Four leaves are missing from Part II, probably including a chapter heading, so 41 surviving chapter containers must not be treated as the certain original chapter count.

related_works.title: The Rule of Christian Religion
notes: Pecock presents the Donet as an introduction, compact report, and complement to the longer Rule; this is a functional relation, not a claim that one surviving manuscript was copied from the other.

related_works.title: The Folewer to the Donet
notes: The Folewer is the planned sequel for more advanced readers and is a separate work.

related_works.title: The Poore Mennis Myrrour
notes: Pecock's separate abridgement extracts and closely follows principal arguments from Part I for poorer and less learned readers. It is not a second manuscript witness of the complete Donet, although Hitchcock used its parallel passages for collation.

open_questions.question: Can the Donet's authorized composition or publication date and original place of composition be established more narrowly than c. 1445, 1443-1449, and an East Midland linguistic association?
next_steps: Consult recent Pecock chronology and dialect scholarship that distinguishes draft circulation, revision, and authorized publication.
Keep the work's composition place separate from the later witness's English origin and present Oxford holding.

open_questions.question: Exactly how much text and how many chapter boundaries were lost with the four missing leaves in Part II?
next_steps: Compare the Donet's internal cross-references, the Rule of Christian Religion, and The Poore Mennis Myrrour against the surviving sequence.
Do not normalize Hitchcock's questioned Part II chapter numbers into a certain original count.

open_questions.question: Can a complete current image sequence of MS. Bodl. 916 confirm every physical boundary, blank, missing-leaf stub, and the difference between the catalog-inferred fols. 1r-109v locus and Hitchcock's fols. 1r-106v written text?
next_steps: Request the complete physical codex from Bodleian Imaging Services or mediated copying, including covers, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, stubs, and every recto and verso, rather than ordering only the Donet text.
If supplied, cache the complete deliverable and map the work text to fols. 1r-106v, retaining any different authoritative image or folio sequence.
Ask for written permission before redistributing supplied images in the Git repository or using them in commercial print-on-demand products.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / CME source XML, CME00008.xml.
evidence.summary: Identifies CME00008, transcribes the 1921 title page and editor's summary, and encodes the surviving prologue and two-part Donet with page, folio, line, editorial-note, and typographic markup.
Counts one prologue, 18 Part I DIV3 chapters, 23 Part II DIV3 chapters, 642 P, 214 PB, 2,299 NOTE, 14,114 HI, and no L or LG elements within the treatise division.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, item CME00008.
evidence.locator: item information, rights statement, contents, and complete digital text
evidence.summary: Identifies Pecock, Hitchcock's 1921 edition, doctrinal theology as subject, the prologue and two-part chapter structure, and a No Copyright-United States rights statement.

evidence.citation: Reginald Pecock, The Donet, ed. Elsie Vaughan Hitchcock, Early English Text Society, Original Series 156 (London: Humphrey Milford, Oxford University Press, 1921).
evidence.locator: title page; introduction pp. xi-xxiv; text pp. 1-214; notes pp. 215-222; Appendix I pp. 223-230
evidence.summary: The immediate edition describes the unique, imperfect Bodl. 916 witness; argues for composition or authorized publication in 1443-1449; explains its transcription and expansion practices; prints the complete surviving work; and documents the separate Poore Mennis Myrrour collation.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, MS. Bodl. 916 (catalog record manuscript_1880).
evidence.summary: Supplies the current shelfmark, sole-work cataloging, explicitly inferred fols. 1r-109v locus, Middle English language, parchment support, 109-leaf extent plus endleaves, four missing leaves after fols. 76 and 82, English origin in the second half of the fifteenth century, and the selected fol. 3v plate in Hitchcock.

evidence.citation: Manuscripts and Archives at Oxford University, MS. Bodl. 916 extract record.
evidence.locator: record identity, contents, requesting section, and online-availability label
evidence.summary: Identifies MS. Bodl. 916 and The Donet, links the full medieval-manuscripts record and request route, and explicitly labels the item 'Not available online.'

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Add MS 37788.
evidence.locator: scope and content, components, date, language, and access
evidence.summary: Identifies the mid-fifteenth-century codex and its first component, Pecock's Poore Mennis Myrrour beginning at fol. 3, as an extract from the first part of the Donet; lists the other contents and provides a physical-item request route without a digitized-content link.

evidence.citation: Middle English Compendium, Middle English Dictionary citations for Pecock Donet (Bod 916).
evidence.summary: The MED citation convention dates the surviving Bodl. 916 copy to c. 1475 and the work represented by it to c. 1445.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Special collections mediated copying.
evidence.locator: service scope, order method, cost, turnaround, exceptions, and usage restrictions
evidence.summary: Offers reference-quality PDF scans of manuscripts to all users, subject to conservation and legal review; lists GBP 1 per scan and a GBP 10 minimum for non-Oxford users; and applies separate reuse terms to supplied files.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries Imaging Services, Order images.
evidence.locator: products, prices, large-order qualification, order procedure, and terms
evidence.summary: Offers high-resolution color TIFF photography by order, publishes a per-image schedule but negotiates large orders, requires precise shelfmark and recto/verso folios, and separates image supply from permission for commercial reuse.

notes: The current Bodleian TEI locus 1r-109v is marked type='inferred' because the codex has one msItem. Hitchcock directly describes 106 written folios plus blank matter, and the encoded folio milestones end at 106v; the exact work-text mapping is therefore fols. 1r-106v while the broader inferred catalog locus is retained as a competing catalog assertion.
The Poore Mennis Myrrour is modeled as a related authorial abridgement and collation source, not as a witness of the complete Donet.
Rights in the medieval work, the 1921 edition, U-M encoding, Internet Archive scan, selected manuscript plate, and any future commissioned Bodleian images remain separate in the lineage manifest.
